question: Explain BLoC pattern: Events, States, Bloc vs Cubit.
answer:
BLoC (Business Logic Component) separates UI from business logic using events and states.

Cubit — simplified BLoC:
• No events — call methods directly to emit new states
• Less boilerplate, good for simple state

Bloc — full event-driven:
• Explicit Event classes → better auditing, logging, undo/redo
• on<Event>() handlers registered in constructor
• More testable, more explicit flow

Both:
• BlocBuilder — rebuild UI based on state
• BlocListener — side effects (navigation, snackbar)
• BlocConsumer — builder + listener combined
• MultiBlocProvider — provide multiple blocs

question: What is BlocObserver and how do you use it for logging and analytics?
answer:
BlocObserver is a global listener for all Bloc/Cubit events, state changes, transitions, and errors. Set it once in main() — it applies to every Bloc in your app.

Use cases:
• Logging all state transitions (debugging)
• Sending analytics events
• Crash reporting (Sentry, Firebase Crashlytics)
• Performance monitoring

override methods:
• onCreate — bloc created
• onEvent — event added (Bloc only)
• onChange — state changed
• onTransition — event → state (Bloc only)
• onError — error occurred
• onClose — bloc closed

question: What are event transformers in BLoC and what do droppable / restartable / sequential / concurrent do?
answer:
Event transformers control HOW events flowing into an `on<Event>` handler are processed. By default, events are processed CONCURRENTLY — multiple in-flight handlers can be running at once. For most flows that's wrong. The bloc_concurrency package ships four standard policies.

concurrent (default):
• Each event spawns its own async handler in parallel
• Latest emit can come from any of them — race conditions in user-facing flows
• Use only when handlers are truly independent and order doesn't matter

sequential:
• Process events one at a time, in arrival order — handler N waits for N-1 to complete
• Use for: write paths where ordering matters (cart updates, save sequences)

droppable:
• If a handler is currently in flight, DROP new events until it finishes
• Use for: "submit" buttons — extra taps shouldn't fire extra requests

restartable:
• When a new event arrives, CANCEL the in-flight handler and start fresh with the new event
• Use for: search-as-you-type, autocomplete, navigation cancellation. Equivalent to switchMap in Rx.

Debouncing on top:
• Combine with rxdart-style debounce: events.debounceTime(300ms).switchMap(mapper)
• bloc_concurrency lets you compose: e.g. pre-debounce events then route to restartable

Gotcha: emit() after a cancel raises StateError. With `restartable`, check `emit.isDone` before emitting in long chains so a cancelled handler doesn't try to emit on a closed emitter.

question: What's the difference between BlocBuilder, BlocSelector, and BlocConsumer (plus buildWhen)?
answer:
All three subscribe to a Bloc/Cubit; they differ in what triggers a rebuild and whether they can run side effects.

BlocBuilder<B, S>:
• Rebuilds the builder on every state change
• Optional `buildWhen: (prev, next) => bool` — gate rebuilds on a custom predicate (returns true to rebuild)
• Use for the common case: "render UI from current state"

BlocSelector<B, S, T>:
• Selects a derived value `T` from state and rebuilds only when `T` changes by ==
• Cleaner when the widget cares about one field of a big state
• Like Provider's Selector or Riverpod's `provider.select`

BlocConsumer<B, S>:
• Combines builder + listener in one widget
• `listener: (context, state) {...}` for side effects (navigation, snackbar) — does NOT cause rebuild
• `listenWhen` and `buildWhen` independently gate listener vs builder calls
• Use when both UI and side-effect logic depend on the same state

Why buildWhen matters:
• Reduces unnecessary rebuilds without splitting the bloc — sometimes coarse state is fine if you predicate the rebuild
• Default: rebuild on every state — wasteful for big states

Guideline: BlocSelector for one-field reads, BlocBuilder + buildWhen for multi-field logic, BlocConsumer when listener is involved. Don't put navigation in builder — that's a render-time side effect, the wrong place.

question: How do BlocProvider, MultiBlocProvider, and BlocProvider.value differ?
answer:
Three ways to inject blocs into the widget tree, each with a specific role.

BlocProvider<B>:
• `BlocProvider(create: (_) => B(), child: ...)`
• Owns the bloc's lifecycle — created when the provider mounts, closed when it unmounts
• Use for: a fresh bloc scoped to a screen or feature subtree

BlocProvider<B>.value:
• `BlocProvider.value(value: existingBloc, child: ...)`
• Reuses an existing bloc instance — does NOT close it on unmount
• Use for: passing a bloc to a child route (e.g., a modal sheet) that needs the same instance, navigating with an already-bootstrapped bloc

MultiBlocProvider:
• Flattens nested BlocProviders into a single declaration with a `providers: [...]` list
• Identical behavior to nesting; just nicer to read
• Order matters: providers earlier in the list are visible to providers after them via `context.read`

Lifecycle gotchas:
• A bloc created by BlocProvider is closed when the widget is unmounted. If you push a new screen with `Navigator.push`, the parent's BlocProvider is still mounted — but a child route pushed without inheriting the provider can't see it. Use `BlocProvider.value(value: context.read<B>(), child: NextScreen())` to forward.
• Don't share a bloc between two unrelated subtrees — each should own its lifecycle. If you really need shared state, that's app-level, not bloc-level.
• `lazy: false` on BlocProvider creates the bloc immediately, useful when the bloc kicks off bootstrap work in its constructor and you want it running before the first frame

For go_router: each `GoRoute` builds a fresh widget tree on navigation; wrap individual routes with their bloc providers, or use a `ShellRoute` with shared providers higher in the tree.

question: How do you persist BLoC state across restarts with hydrated_bloc?
answer:
Out of the box, blocs lose state when the app restarts. `hydrated_bloc` adds JSON serialization to local storage for any subset of your blocs — perfect for theme, language, draft text, last-used filters.

How it works:
• Extend HydratedBloc / HydratedCubit instead of Bloc / Cubit
• Implement `fromJson(Map<String, dynamic>)` and `toJson(State)` — return null from toJson to skip persistence for that state
• HydratedStorage is initialized once in main() with a directory (typically `getApplicationDocumentsDirectory`)
• The current state is automatically saved after every state change and rehydrated on next app launch

When to persist (and when not to):
• Persist: theme, language, onboarding status, draft text, last filter, last route, simple non-sensitive flags
• Don't persist: anything that could be stale and harmful (auth state should be derived from a fresh token, not a cached AuthSuccess), any sensitive data (use flutter_secure_storage instead), large data (use a real database)

Gotchas:
• Schema migrations: if the State shape changes, fromJson must handle old shapes or return null to fall back to initial. Bumping a version field inside the JSON is the easiest mitigation.
• Errors during fromJson are silent by default — log them; otherwise you'll silently regress to initial state and not understand why
• Test mode: HydratedStorage.storage = HiveDirectoryStorage(directory: ...) for tests; or override per-test

For app-wide preferences not tied to a bloc, plain SharedPreferences or flutter_secure_storage may be a simpler fit — hydrated_bloc shines when the data IS the bloc state.

question: How do you test a Bloc with bloc_test?
answer:
bloc_test ships a `blocTest` helper that scripts a bloc through events and asserts the resulting state stream. It's the standard way to unit-test bloc business logic with no widget tree.

Setup:
• `blocTest<B, S>(...)` — build the bloc, optionally seed initial state, fire events, assert states
• `act` — function that calls bloc.add or cubit method
• `expect` — list of states (or matchers) the bloc should emit
• `wait` — hold for async transformers (debounce, switchMap)
• `verify` — additional assertions after `expect`
• `setUp` / `tearDown` for shared mocks

Key patterns:
• Use `mocktail` to fake repositories. Stub `when(() => repo.fetch()).thenAnswer((_) async => [...])`
• For sealed-state hierarchies, use isA<T>() matchers to assert types without comparing every field
• Test order-sensitive events with sequential transformers — bloc_test waits for them deterministically
• Snap a sealed-state's full equality via `equatable` or `freezed` so `expect` can compare value-by-value

For ground truth on UI-level effects, complement with widget tests using BlocProvider.value over a fake bloc.

question: How do you communicate between Blocs without coupling them?
answer:
Blocs should not directly call each other — that creates a tight coupling that's hard to test and reason about. There are three accepted patterns to coordinate behavior.

1) Listen and react via BlocListener:
• In a widget, place a BlocListener<A, AState> that, on a specific transition, calls `bloc<B>().add(...)`
• Keeps both blocs unaware of each other; the WIDGET orchestrates them
• Best for UI-level coordination (auth changes → reset cart, language changes → reload feed)

2) Subscribe to another bloc's stream inside a bloc:
• A bloc can `subscribe()` to another bloc's `stream` in its constructor and add internal events as a result
• Tighter coupling than option 1, but no widget mediation needed
• Cancel the subscription in `close()` to avoid leaks

3) Shared repository / service:
• Both blocs depend on a common service (RepositoryProvider) that emits a stream of changes
• Each bloc reacts independently to the service's stream
• Cleanest decoupling — the blocs don't know each other exists
• Best for cross-feature state (user session, sync engine status, network connectivity)

Guideline: prefer (3) for app-wide signals, (1) for screen-level coordination, (2) only when (1) and (3) don't fit.

Never do: import bloc B from inside bloc A and call methods on it. That makes A untestable without B and creates a directed dependency you'll regret.

question: How do you handle errors in a Bloc — try/catch, addError, and onError?
answer:
Error handling in BLoC has three layers, each with its own purpose.

1) try/catch inside the handler:
• Standard Dart — the right place to translate a thrown exception into an emitted error state
• Lets you keep the bloc emitting valid states (`Failure(...)`) instead of letting an unhandled exception bubble up
• Use for: expected failures (network down, validation mismatch, 4xx responses)

2) addError(error, stackTrace):
• A method on Bloc/Cubit that records an error WITHOUT changing state
• Useful when you want to log/report the error but the user-visible state has already moved on
• Calls flow into the bloc's `onError` and BlocObserver.onError
• Use sparingly — when an exception is unexpected and you want telemetry but no UI change

3) Override onError in the Bloc / globally in BlocObserver:
• Bloc.onError — last-chance hook for THIS bloc
• Bloc.observer.onError — application-wide; great for crashlytics, sentry
• Always call super.onError so the chain continues

Design rules:
• Model expected failures as state. UI knows how to render `Failure(message)` — let try/catch translate exceptions into them.
• Reserve `addError` and BlocObserver.onError for unexpected exceptions and telemetry.
• Never let a handler throw silently. If it does, the bloc enters a broken state and subsequent emits may behave oddly.
• On hot reload during development, an unhandled error can wipe state in surprising ways — guard with try/catch even in early prototypes.

BlocObserver is also where you forward errors to Crashlytics/Sentry — set it up once in main and you have a single capture point for all bloc-level errors.
